methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

transformations: TGT amount → TITN amount via time-decayed conversion rate
data_flows.trust_changes: Trusts TGT contract to supply correct 'from' address; no verification of from parameter integrity beyond msg.sender == TGT address
exposure_risks: No maximum TGT deposit cap enforcement - total TGT deposited can exceed TGT_TO_EXCHANGE
titnOut can be 0 at boundary (timeSinceLaunch approaching 360d) while deposit still succeeds
manipulation_risks: Total TITN allocated can exceed TITN_ARB if deposited TGT exceeds TGT_TO_EXCHANGE causing insolvency
No minimum output protection - user deposits TGT but may receive 0 TITN near 360d boundary
Front-running: attacker monitors mempool for deposits and front-runs to exhaust TITN allocation

transformations: Direct TITN transfer equal to claimed amount
data_flows.trust_changes: Contract trusts that TITN balance is sufficient for transfer
exposure_risks: If totalTitnClaimable exceeds actual TITN balance (due to over-allocation from unlimited TGT deposits), safeTransfer will revert for later claimants
manipulation_risks: First-come-first-served: if TITN is over-allocated, early claimers drain the contract leaving nothing for later users
No launchTime > 0 check; relies on inability to have claimable amounts if launchTime is 0

path: First caller initializes remainingTitnAfter1Year and initialTotalClaimable
transformations: Bonus: proportional share of (contractBalance - totalClaimable) based on user's share of totalClaimable
data_flows.trust_changes: First caller sets immutable snapshot values that affect all subsequent callers
exposure_risks: remainingTitnAfter1Year is set by first caller - race condition on initialization
Owner can withdraw() TITN before initialization, corrupting proportional calculation
manipulation_risks: Owner drains TITN via withdraw() before first withdrawRemainingTitn call causing underflow in unclaimedTitn calculation
First caller can be manipulated/front-run to set unfavorable snapshot
If owner re-deposits TITN between initialization and subsequent user claims, contract balance grows but snapshot is fixed

path: Source chain: OFT._debit() → _burn(sender, amount) [bypasses transfer restrictions]
transformations: Token burned on source, minted on destination
Recipient permanently flagged as bridged token holder
data_flows.trust_changes: Recipient becomes permanently restricted sender on non-Arbitrum chains; on Arbitrum all non-exempt senders already restricted
validation_points: LayerZero endpoint authentication in OFT base
exposure_risks: isBridgedTokenHolder flag is permanent - no function to clear it
Bridging to any chain marks recipient, restricting them even after they dispose of all bridged tokens
manipulation_risks: Attacker can bridge small amount to arbitrary address to permanently flag them as bridged token holder, restricting their transfers on non-Arbitrum chains
Address(0) redirect to 0xdead in _credit - could be used to permanently lock tokens if LayerZero misconfigures _to

exposure_risks: Owner can withdraw ALL TITN at any time including user-claimable amounts
No restriction on token type or amount - can drain TGT and TITN
Can be used before withdrawRemainingTitn initialization to corrupt proportional math
manipulation_risks: Rug pull vector: owner drains TITN, users cannot claim
Targeted attack: owner partially drains to cause underflow in withdrawRemainingTitn

exposure_risks: Only one transferAllowedContract at a time - other integrations are blocked
lzEndpoint check is likely ineffective for OFT bridging (which uses _burn internally)
manipulation_risks: On Arbitrum, all users are restricted regardless of how they obtained tokens (native or bridged)
Anyone can force-flag an address as isBridgedTokenHolder by bridging dust to them

core_rules: TGT to TITN conversion follows a time-decayed rate: full rate for 90 days, linear decay over next 270 days to zero
Users deposit TGT and accumulate claimable TITN, which they claim separately
After 360 days, unclaimed TITN plus bonus from unallocated TITN is distributed proportionally
Bridged token holders on non-Arbitrum chains and all holders on Arbitrum have transfer restrictions
Owner has complete control over lock status, transfer allowed contracts, and can withdraw any token
economic_incentives: Early TGT depositors get better conversion rate (full rate in first 90 days)
Linear decay from day 90 to day 360 incentivizes earlier participation
Post-360-day proportional bonus incentivizes NOT claiming before 360 days (to get bonus share)
Transfer restrictions on bridged tokens prevent immediate selling on destination chains
governance_mechanisms: Owner controls LockedStatus (Locked/OneWay/TwoWay) - only OneWay and TwoWay allow deposits
Owner controls transferAllowedContract and bridgedTokenTransferLocked on TITN
Owner can withdraw any amount of any token from MergeTgt at any time

edge_cases.scenario: Total TGT deposited exceeds TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (579M TGT), causing total TITN allocated to exceed TITN_ARB
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Contract should enforce a cap on total TGT accepted or total TITN allocated
edge_cases.actual_behavior: No cap is enforced. Any amount of TGT can be deposited. totalTitnClaimable can exceed TITN_ARB, making the contract insolvent
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If total TGT deposited * rate > TITN_ARB, early claimers drain all TITN; late claimers get nothing. Also causes underflow in withdrawRemainingTitn (remainingTitnAfter1Year < initialTotalClaimable)

edge_cases.scenario: User deposits TGT when timeSinceLaunch is very close to but less than 360 days
edge_cases.expected_behavior: User should be protected from receiving negligible TITN for their TGT
edge_cases.actual_behavior: quoteTitn returns near-zero value; user's TGT is locked in contract with effectively zero TITN claimable. No minimum output check exists
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: User loses TGT with no meaningful TITN return. No slippage protection.

edge_cases.scenario: At exactly block.timestamp - launchTime == 360 days, onTokenTransfer allows deposit but quoteTitn returns 0
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Deposit should be rejected if output is 0
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Deposit is accepted (360 days is NOT > 360 days). quoteTitn returns 0 because timeSinceLaunch is not < 360 days. User loses TGT for 0 TITN
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Off-by-one at the boundary: deposits at exactly 360 days are accepted but yield 0 TITN

edge_cases.scenario: Owner calls withdraw() to drain TITN before first withdrawRemainingTitn call
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Contract should protect user funds from owner extraction of allocated TITN
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Owner can drain all TITN. When first user calls withdrawRemainingTitn, remainingTitnAfter1Year could be 0 or very low, causing underflow when computing unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Rug pull: owner drains TITN before or after 360 days, rendering all claims impossible

edge_cases.scenario: isBridgedTokenHolder flag set permanently with no way to clear it
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Flag should be clearable when user is no longer holding bridged tokens, or by admin
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Once _credit is called for an address, isBridgedTokenHolder[_to] = true forever. No function exists to reset it
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attacker bridges dust amount to target address on non-Arbitrum chain, permanently restricting target's ability to transfer TITN when bridgedTokenTransferLocked is true. Griefing attack.

edge_cases.scenario: withdrawRemainingTitn called when remainingTitnAfter1Year is 0 (first caller when balance is 0)
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Should handle gracefully or revert with meaningful error
edge_cases.actual_behavior: remainingTitnAfter1Year stays 0, if-check will re-execute on every call. unclaimedTitn = 0 - initialTotalClaimable underflows and reverts if initialTotalClaimable > 0. Function is permanently broken
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If contract balance is 0 at 360 days (e.g., owner drained), the initialization sets remainingTitnAfter1Year=0 which causes perpetual re-initialization attempts that all revert

edge_cases.expected_behavior: totalTitnClaimed and totalTitnClaimable should reflect actual movements accurately
edge_cases.actual_behavior: totalTitnClaimed += titnOut (includes proportional bonus), but totalTitnClaimable -= claimableTitn (only base amount). The proportional bonus is never tracked in totalTitnClaimable, creating a divergence between claimed and claimable totals
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Accounting inconsistency; not directly exploitable but could mislead off-chain monitoring or integrations relying on these values

edge_cases.scenario: LockedStatus.TwoWay is defined but never functionally differs from OneWay in code
edge_cases.expected_behavior: TwoWay should enable additional functionality (e.g., reverse exchange from TITN to TGT)
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Both OneWay and TwoWay allow deposits. No code path differentiates them. The TwoWay enum value is unused
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: No immediate exploitation, but suggests incomplete implementation or dead code

edge_cases.scenario: Multiple users race to be first caller of withdrawRemainingTitn - snapshot values depend on exact state at time of first call
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Snapshot should be deterministic and fair
edge_cases.actual_behavior: First caller sets remainingTitnAfter1Year and initialTotalClaimable. If someone claims via claimTitn right before 360d, this reduces both balance and totalTitnClaimable consistently. But owner actions (withdraw/deposit) can create inconsistencies
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Owner or MEV bot can manipulate contract state in the same block as initialization to set favorable/unfavorable snapshot values

edge_cases.scenario: On Arbitrum, all TITN transfers are restricted regardless of whether sender is a bridged token holder
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Only bridged token holders should be restricted, or restriction should be clearly documented
edge_cases.actual_behavior: block.chainid == 42161 causes ALL transfers to be restricted on Arbitrum. Even tokens minted natively via constructor on Arbitrum deployment would be restricted
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Users who receive TITN on Arbitrum via the MergeTgt contract (not bridging) are still transfer-restricted unless transferAllowedContract is set to MergeTgt

edge_cases.scenario: deposit() function can be called multiple times by owner
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Deposit should only be allowed once for initial TITN funding
edge_cases.actual_behavior: No state tracking prevents multiple deposits. Owner can deposit TITN_ARB multiple times, increasing balance beyond expected amount
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Owner can over-fund the contract, changing bonus distribution math in withdrawRemainingTitn. Not harmful to users but inconsistent with the constant naming.

assumption_violations: CRITICAL: Contract assumes total TGT deposited will not exceed TGT_TO_EXCHANGE, but this is never enforced. If total TGT supply allows more deposits, the contract becomes insolvent
Contract assumes owner will not withdraw user-allocated TITN, but withdraw() has no restrictions
Transfer restriction on Titn assumes isBridgedTokenHolder accurately reflects current bridged status, but flag is permanent and one-directional
Contract assumes first withdrawRemainingTitn caller will execute when contract has a positive TITN balance >= totalTitnClaimable, but owner can drain beforehand
to != lzEndpoint check assumes users need to transfer to lzEndpoint, but OFT uses internal _burn which bypasses transfer/transferFrom entirely
game_theory_risks: Perverse incentive: users who do NOT claim before 360 days get a proportional bonus from unallocated TITN in withdrawRemainingTitn, discouraging early claims
If insolvency risk is known, rational actors race to claimTitn early, creating a bank-run scenario where late users get nothing
Griefing via isBridgedTokenHolder: attacker can cheaply bridge dust to arbitrary addresses on non-Arbitrum chains to permanently restrict their TITN transfers
MEV opportunity: at exactly 360 days, the claimTitn→withdrawRemainingTitn transition creates potential for block-level manipulation of the initialization snapshot
integration_risks: LayerZero endpoint trust: _credit relies on LayerZero authentication; compromise of LZ infrastructure allows arbitrary minting
ERC677 transferAndCall: relies on TGT token correctly implementing the callback pattern and passing authentic 'from' address
Single transferAllowedContract: only one contract can be whitelisted at a time. If MergeTgt is set as transferAllowedContract, no DEX can trade TITN, and vice versa
OFT _debit uses _burn which bypasses transfer restrictions - this is likely intended but means bridge-out is always possible even when transfers are locked
lzEndpoint stored in both OFT base and Titn private variable - if endpoint implementation is upgraded (address change), the two references could diverge
calculation_errors: quoteTitn at timeSinceLaunch = 360 days returns 0 (correct per math), but onTokenTransfer allows deposits at exactly 360 days (boundary inconsistency between > and >= checks)
withdrawRemainingTitn: unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable can underflow if contract was drained or if over-allocation occurred
withdrawRemainingTitn: integer division truncation in userProportionalShare means sum of all shares is slightly less than unclaimedTitn, leaving dust TITN permanently locked
quoteTitn full rate: tgtAmount * 173_700_000e18 / 579_000_000e18 ≈ 0.3 * tgtAmount - calculation is correct but very small TGT amounts may result in 0 due to integer division

token_semantics.notes: External token. ERC677 pattern used for deposits. Trusted to provide correct 'from' address in onTokenTransfer callback. No fee-on-transfer assumed.

LayerZero OFT token. Transfer restrictions based on isBridgedTokenHolder flag and chain ID. _mint/_burn bypass transfer restrictions. isBridgedTokenHolder is permanently set on _credit. On Arbitrum (chainId 42161), ALL transfers are restricted. Owner and transferAllowedContract exempted.

invariants.name: claimable_sum_consistency
invariants.notes: totalTitnClaimable should equal sum of all claimableTitnPerUser values. Both are incremented/decremented in sync in onTokenTransfer and claimTitn. In withdrawRemainingTitn, totalTitnClaimable -= claimableTitn while user is zeroed, maintaining consistency.

invariants.name: solvency_invariant
invariants.notes: VIOLATED if total TGT deposited exceeds TGT_TO_EXCHANGE. No enforcement prevents totalTitnClaimable from exceeding actual TITN balance. Also violated if owner uses withdraw().

invariants.name: single_withdrawal_post_year
invariants.notes: In withdrawRemainingTitn, claimableTitnPerUser[msg.sender] is set to 0 after claim. User can only call once since subsequent calls fail require(claimableTitn > 0).

invariants.name: temporal_phase_exclusivity
invariants.notes: Before 360 days: claimTitn active, withdrawRemainingTitn blocked. At/after 360 days: claimTitn blocked, withdrawRemainingTitn active. Clean transition at exactly 360 days.

invariants.name: bridged_flag_monotonicity
invariants.notes: isBridgedTokenHolder can only transition from false to true, never back. No clearing mechanism exists.
